title: Onion Kulcha (Tawa)

subs.name: half atta half maida for a wholegrain version

steps.text: Combine maida, baking soda, baking powder, sugar, salt and oil in a bowl. Add curd and mix. Add lukewarm water a little at a time to form a soft, slightly sticky dough. Knead for 5 minutes until smooth. Cover with a damp cloth and rest for at least 1 hour (or overnight in the fridge for the best flavour).

Make the filling: combine chopped onion, green chilli, coriander leaves, amchur and cumin seeds in a bowl. Add a pinch of salt and mix. Squeeze the mixture lightly so the onion releases some moisture, then drain off that moisture so the filling is not watery.

Divide the rested dough into 8 portions. Roll one into a 10 cm disc, place 2 tbsp of onion filling in the centre, gather the edges and pinch shut. Flatten gently and roll out to an oval or round about 16 cm wide — kulchas are typically oval.
Heat a heavy tawa over high flame until very hot. Wet one side of the kulcha with a little water and place the wet side down on the tawa. Press gently with the back of a spoon.

Cook for about 90 seconds until the surface looks dry and bubbles start to form. Flip and cook the second side for 60 seconds until golden brown patches appear. If your tawa allows it, invert the tawa over an open flame to char the top side lightly — this mimics the tandoor.
Brush immediately with butter and serve hot with chole, dal makhani or a simple curd. The butter is not optional — it is what makes a kulcha a kulcha.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.

substitution_notes: maida → half atta half maida for a wholegrain version; curd → plant-based yogurt for dairy-free; butter → margarine or oil
